parts.text:
You run a two-factory manufacturing pipeline. Factory A makes components, then Factory B finishes the same jobs.
Each factory is its own job shop with one-job-per-machine exclusivity and no preemption.
Every job must finish all of its Factory A operations before that same job may start any Factory B operation.
Your objective is to minimize the coupled makespan: the time when the final Factory B operation finishes.

Instance size: 6 jobs, 7 machines in each factory.
Jobs and ordered operations:
- Job 1: Factory A MA7 (7) -> MA1 (9) -> MA6 (6) -> MA5 (9) -> MA2 (8) -> MA4 (9) -> MA3 (5); Factory B MB3 (1) -> MB5 (1) -> MB2 (6) -> MB1 (8) -> MB7 (6) -> MB4 (7) -> MB6 (7)
- Job 2: Factory A MA5 (8) -> MA2 (7) -> MA7 (9) -> MA6 (6) -> MA1 (6) -> MA3 (6) -> MA4 (8); Factory B MB2 (3) -> MB3 (7) -> MB7 (8) -> MB5 (9) -> MB6 (4) -> MB4 (8) -> MB1 (5)
- Job 3: Factory A MA4 (3) -> MA5 (5) -> MA6 (8) -> MA3 (5) -> MA7 (5) -> MA2 (9) -> MA1 (9); Factory B MB3 (9) -> MB5 (9) -> MB6 (7) -> MB4 (5) -> MB2 (4) -> MB1 (8) -> MB7 (9)
- Job 4: Factory A MA3 (9) -> MA6 (3) -> MA5 (5) -> MA1 (4) -> MA2 (4) -> MA4 (1) -> MA7 (7); Factory B MB6 (1) -> MB1 (1) -> MB7 (6) -> MB5 (6) -> MB2 (3) -> MB4 (4) -> MB3 (1)
- Job 5: Factory A MA1 (1) -> MA7 (4) -> MA6 (3) -> MA5 (1) -> MA4 (1) -> MA3 (6) -> MA2 (2); Factory B MB3 (5) -> MB2 (6) -> MB6 (8) -> MB5 (1) -> MB7 (5) -> MB1 (8) -> MB4 (9)
- Job 6: Factory A MA7 (9) -> MA5 (7) -> MA1 (8) -> MA3 (9) -> MA2 (6) -> MA6 (3) -> MA4 (6); Factory B MB2 (5) -> MB1 (5) -> MB3 (7) -> MB6 (1) -> MB4 (9) -> MB7 (3) -> MB5 (1)

Constraints:
- A machine handles at most one job at a time.
- Each job must respect its listed operation order inside each factory.
- Factory B job Jx cannot start until Factory A job Jx has fully completed.
- When you emit a schedule, every required operation must appear exactly once.

A deterministic feasible baseline schedule is provided below. If you stop immediately or fail to produce a valid schedule, this baseline is what gets scored.
Baseline makespan: 255
Baseline schedule summary:
Factory A: MA1: J1[7-16],J2[61-67],J3[116-125],J4[125-129],J5[129-130],J6[161-169]; MA2: J1[31-39],J2[39-46],J3[107-116],J4[129-133],J5[156-158],J6[178-184]; MA3: J1[48-53],J2[67-73],J3[97-102],J4[102-111],J5[150-156],J6[169-178]; MA4: J1[39-48],J2[73-81],J3[81-84],J4[133-134],J5[149-150],J6[187-193]; MA5: J1[22-31],J2[31-39],J3[84-89],J4[114-119],J5[148-149],J6[154-161]; MA6: J1[16-22],J2[55-61],J3[89-97],J4[111-114],J5[145-148],J6[184-187]; MA7: J1[0-7],J2[46-55],J3[102-107],J4[134-141],J5[141-145],J6[145-154]
Factory B: MB1: J1[61-69],J2[120-125],J3[159-167],J4[167-168],J5[221-229],J6[229-234]; MB2: J1[55-61],J2[81-84],J3[155-159],J4[188-191],J5[201-207],J6[207-212]; MB3: J1[53-54],J2[84-91],J3[125-134],J4[195-196],J5[196-201],J6[234-241]; MB4: J1[75-82],J2[112-120],J3[150-155],J4[191-195],J5[229-238],J6[242-251]; MB5: J1[54-55],J2[99-108],J3[134-143],J4[182-188],J5[215-216],J6[254-255]; MB6: J1[82-89],J2[108-112],J3[143-150],J4[150-151],J5[207-215],J6[241-242]; MB7: J1[69-75],J2[91-99],J3[167-176],J4[176-182],J5[216-221],J6[251-254]
Makespan: 255


FULL PRIOR TRANSCRIPT:
TURN_1_MODEL_RESPONSE:
PLAN_STATE:
1. Determine job priority for Factory A: J5, J3, J2, J1, J6, J4 (prioritizing short A-time and/or long B-time).
2. Construct Factory A schedule using a greedy approach with the above priorities.
3. Calculate the release time C_A(Jx) for each job.
4. Construct Factory B schedule using release times from Factory A as start-time constraints.
5. Prioritize Factory B operations based on their earliest possible start time and remaining work.
6. Identify the critical path in the combined schedule.
7. Iteratively refine machine sequences to reduce idle time on bottleneck machines (MA7, MB4, MB7).
8. Ensure all Factory B operations start after their corresponding Factory A job completion.

NEXT_SUB: {"id": 1, "desc": "Construct a complete schedule for both Factory A and Factory B using a priority-based list-scheduling approach and manual refinement.", "p_solve": 0.60, "time_budget_s": 300}

TURN_2_MODEL_RESPONSE:
SUB_1: 1. Job Priorities: Identified J5, J4, J3, J1, J2, J6 as an efficient sequence for Factory A to minimize early release times for Factory B.
2. Factory A Schedule: Built a feasible schedule with release times: J5(18), J4(33), J1(69), J3(69), J6(93), J2(101).
3. Factory B Schedule: Used release times as start constraints. MB3, MB2, and MB6 were initial bottlenecks, but optimized sequencing kept the makespan low.
4. Makespan: Achieved a total makespan of 151, a significant reduction from the baseline 255.
5. Verification: All machine exclusivity, job operation order, and Factory A -> Factory B constraints have been verified.
BEST_GUESS: {"factory_a": {"MA1": [["J5", 0, 1], ["J4", 17, 21], ["J1", 21, 30], ["J6", 52, 60], ["J3", 60, 69], ["J2", 69, 75]], "MA2": [["J5", 16, 18], ["J4", 21, 25], ["J2", 25, 32], ["J3", 38, 47], ["J1", 47, 55], ["J6", 78, 84]], "MA3": [["J4", 0, 9], ["J5", 10, 16], ["J3", 20, 25], ["J1", 64, 69], ["J6", 69, 78], ["J2", 78, 84]], "MA4": [["J3", 0, 3], ["J5", 9, 10], ["J4", 25, 26], ["J1", 55, 64], ["J6", 87, 93], ["J2", 93, 101]], "MA5": [["J3", 3, 8], ["J5", 8, 9], ["J4", 12, 17], ["J2", 17, 25], ["J1", 36, 45], ["J6", 45, 52]], "MA6": [["J5", 5, 8], ["J4", 9, 12], ["J3", 12, 20], ["J1", 30, 36], ["J2", 47, 53], ["J6", 84, 87]], "MA7": [["J5", 1, 5], ["J1", 5, 12], ["J6", 12, 21], ["J4", 26, 33], ["J3", 33, 38], ["J2", 38, 47]]}, "factory_b": {"MB1": [["J5", 43, 51], ["J4", 51, 52], ["J1", 80, 88], ["J6", 98, 103], ["J3", 124, 132], ["J2", 146, 151]], "MB2": [["J5", 23, 29], ["J4", 64, 67], ["J1", 74, 80], ["J6", 93, 98], ["J2", 101, 104], ["J3", 120, 124]], "MB3": [["J5", 18, 23], ["J4", 71, 72], ["J1", 72, 73], ["J3", 73, 82], ["J6", 103, 110], ["J2", 110, 117]], "MB4": [["J5", 51, 60], ["J4", 67, 71], ["J1", 94, 101], ["J3", 115, 120], ["J6", 120, 129], ["J2", 138, 146]], "MB5": [["J5", 37, 38], ["J4", 58, 64], ["J1", 73, 74], ["J3", 82, 91], ["J2", 125, 134], ["J6", 144, 145]], "MB6": [["J5", 29, 37], ["J4", 37, 38], ["J1", 101, 108], ["J3", 108, 115], ["J6", 115, 116], ["J2", 134, 138]], "MB7": [["J5", 38, 43], ["J4", 52, 58], ["J1", 88, 94], ["J2", 117, 125], ["J3", 132, 141], ["J6", 141, 144]]}, "makespan": 151}
UPDATED_PLAN_STATE: 1. Maintain the current feasible schedule with makespan 151.
2. Analyze the critical path in Factory B starting from J2's final operation on MB1.
3. Explore if shifting J2 or J3 operations in Factory A could further reduce their release times without delaying other jobs.
4. Try to re-sequence Factory B operations on machines MB1 and MB4 to see if the end of the schedule can be compressed.
5. If no further improvements are found within the next 300s, stop.
QUALITY_FORECAST: {"p_gap_le_2": 0.5, "p_gap_le_5": 0.8, "p_gap_le_10": 0.95}
CONTINUE_FORECAST: {"p_improve": 0.2, "expected_delta_score": 2.0, "expected_gap_reduction": 3.0}
DECISION: stop

TIMING: global elapsed 150.8s / 1800s (remaining 1649.2s).
LAST_SUBTASK: budgeted 300s, actual 111.25219995600037s, desc=Construct a complete schedule for both Factory A and Factory B using a priority-based list-scheduling approach and manual refinement.

Exec turn 3. Emit, in order:
- `SUB_2`: key findings for this subtask — HARD LIMIT 30 lines. Stop writing SUB_2 after 30 lines even if incomplete. BEST_GUESS and the remaining fields MUST follow.
- `BEST_GUESS`: class-specific JSON for `cjs`
- BEST_GUESS schema example for `cjs`:
```json
{"factory_a": {"MA1": [["J1", 0, 3], ...]}, "factory_b": {"MB1": [["J1", 3, 5], ...]}, "makespan": 42}.
```
- `UPDATED_PLAN_STATE`: revised plan, or keep the prior plan verbatim
- `QUALITY_FORECAST`: `{p_gap_le_2, p_gap_le_5, p_gap_le_10}` for solo classes, or the VE-specific thresholds if this is `ve`
- `CONTINUE_FORECAST`: `{p_improve, expected_delta_score}` and optionally `expected_gap_reduction`
- `DECISION`: `continue` or `stop`
- `NEXT_SUB`: `{id: 3, desc, p_solve, time_budget_s}` only if you continue

Example shape:
SUB_2: I checked one concrete local move and kept the best valid candidate found.
BEST_GUESS: {"assignment": {"N01": 1, "N02": 2, "N03": 3}}
UPDATED_PLAN_STATE: Keep a valid structured answer at every turn, then spend one more subtask on the highest-value local improvement.
QUALITY_FORECAST: {"p_gap_le_2": 0.05, "p_gap_le_5": 0.2, "p_gap_le_10": 0.55}
CONTINUE_FORECAST: {"p_improve": 0.35, "expected_delta_score": 1.8, "expected_gap_reduction": 4.0}
DECISION: continue
NEXT_SUB: {"id": 3, "desc": "Try one more improvement move", "p_solve": 0.3, "time_budget_s": 120}

Do NOT wrap `PLAN_STATE`, `NEXT_SUB`, `UPDATED_PLAN_STATE`, `DECISION`, `BEST_GUESS`, `QUALITY_FORECAST`, or `CONTINUE_FORECAST` in markdown headers (`##`, `#`), bullets (`-`, `*`), or code fences (```). Emit each as a literal line starting with `<LABEL>:` followed by the value.
CORRECT: UPDATED_PLAN_STATE: Keep a valid structured answer at every turn.
WRONG:   ## UPDATED_PLAN_STATE
Keep a valid structured answer at every turn.  (markdown header — will not parse)
WRONG:   - BEST_GUESS: {"assignment": {"N01": 1, "N02": 2}}   (bullet prefix — will not parse)

All labelled fields must be emitted in the listed order. The `BEST_GUESS:` line must contain valid JSON, with no code fences and no extra text inside that field. Failing to emit any of `BEST_GUESS`, `UPDATED_PLAN_STATE`, `QUALITY_FORECAST`, `CONTINUE_FORECAST`, `DECISION`, or, when you continue, `NEXT_SUB` causes strict-parse failure, and no score is recorded for the turn. Keep each field concise enough that you still emit the full labelled output.
Counterfactual branch: take exactly one more execution turn from the same transcript state. Do not emit `DECISION` or `NEXT_SUB` on this branch.

You are solving an optimization problem.

Value. Each problem is worth up to 100 points of value. Your gap to the optimum reduces value linearly, at 1 point per percentage point of gap, floored at 0.

Time cost. Wall time costs 0.01 points per second.

Budgets. Total wall budget is 1800s. Turn 1 planning budget is 300s. Each execution subtask may budget up to 600s. The harness reserves 300s for a forced one-more-turn counterfactual branch after a clean stop.

Protocol. You may decompose the problem into subtasks, revise your plan each turn, and stop when you judge no more work is worth its time cost. The harness extracts structured fields from your raw text, so when a field expects JSON, emit valid JSON.

Output budget. Your response is token-limited. Keep SUB_N sections under 30 lines — key moves and result only. You MUST reach all structured fields (BEST_GUESS, UPDATED_PLAN_STATE, QUALITY_FORECAST, CONTINUE_FORECAST, DECISION) in every exec turn.

Do not call tools. Do not write code, pseudocode, or solver sketches. Work only from the prompt, the running transcript, the current best artifact, and your own reasoning.

- Job 6: Factory A MA7 (9) -> MA5 (7) -> MA1 (8) -> MA3 (9) -> MA2 (6) -> MA6 (3) -> MA4 (6); Factory B MB2 (5) -> MB1 (5) -> MB3 (7) -> MB6 (1) -> MB4 (9) -> MB7 (3) -> MB5 (1)

Turn 1 for Coupled Job-Shop is planning only. Do not emit BEST_GUESS yet.

Emit, in order:
- `PLAN_STATE`: plan (≤ 15 lines). We will quote this back to you verbatim on every later turn.
- `NEXT_SUB`: `{id: 1, desc, p_solve, time_budget_s}`. `p_solve` is your probability that the final answer lands within the target gap after this subtask. `time_budget_s` must be <= 600.

You run a two-factory manufacturing pipeline. Factory A makes components, then Factory B finishes the same jobs.
Each factory is its own job shop with one-job-per-machine exclusivity and no preemption.
Every job must finish all of its Factory A operations before that same job may start any Factory B operation.
Your objective is to minimize the coupled makespan: the time when the final Factory B operation finishes.

Instance size: 6 jobs, 7 machines in each factory.
Jobs and ordered operations:
- Job 1: Factory A MA7 (7) -> MA1 (9) -> MA6 (6) -> MA5 (9) -> MA2 (8) -> MA4 (9) -> MA3 (5); Factory B MB3 (1) -> MB5 (1) -> MB2 (6) -> MB1 (8) -> MB7 (6) -> MB4 (7) -> MB6 (7)
- Job 2: Factory A MA5 (8) -> MA2 (7) -> MA7 (9) -> MA6 (6) -> MA1 (6) -> MA3 (6) -> MA4 (8); Factory B MB2 (3) -> MB3 (7) -> MB7 (8) -> MB5 (9) -> MB6 (4) -> MB4 (8) -> MB1 (5)
- Job 3: Factory A MA4 (3) -> MA5 (5) -> MA6 (8) -> MA3 (5) -> MA7 (5) -> MA2 (9) -> MA1 (9); Factory B MB3 (9) -> MB5 (9) -> MB6 (7) -> MB4 (5) -> MB2 (4) -> MB1 (8) -> MB7 (9)
- Job 4: Factory A MA3 (9) -> MA6 (3) -> MA5 (5) -> MA1 (4) -> MA2 (4) -> MA4 (1) -> MA7 (7); Factory B MB6 (1) -> MB1 (1) -> MB7 (6) -> MB5 (6) -> MB2 (3) -> MB4 (4) -> MB3 (1)
- Job 5: Factory A MA1 (1) -> MA7 (4) -> MA6 (3) -> MA5 (1) -> MA4 (1) -> MA3 (6) -> MA2 (2); Factory B MB3 (5) -> MB2 (6) -> MB6 (8) -> MB5 (1) -> MB7 (5) -> MB1 (8) -> MB4 (9)
- Job 6: Factory A MA7 (9) -> MA5 (7) -> MA1 (8) -> MA3 (9) -> MA2 (6) -> MA6 (3) -> MA4 (6); Factory B MB2 (5) -> MB1 (5) -> MB3 (7) -> MB6 (1) -> MB4 (9) -> MB7 (3) -> MB5 (1)

Constraints:
- A machine handles at most one job at a time.
- Each job must respect its listed operation order inside each factory.
- Factory B job Jx cannot start until Factory A job Jx has fully completed.
- When you emit a schedule, every required operation must appear exactly once.

A deterministic feasible baseline schedule is provided below. If you stop immediately or fail to produce a valid schedule, this baseline is what gets scored.

taskVersion.definition:
@kbench.task(name=TASK_NAME)
    def detailed_run(
        llm,
        id,
        class_,
        difficulty,
        seed,
        instance,
        gold_objective,
        baseline_objective,
        value_cap,
        wall_budget_s=1800,
        components=None,
    ):
        del wall_budget_s
        inst = module._coerce_json(instance, default=None)
        if not isinstance(inst, dict):
            raise TypeError("instance must be a dict or JSON object string")
        normalized_components = module._normalize_components(components)

        row = module.run_instance(
            llm=llm,
            instance=inst,
            cls=str(class_),
            difficulty=str(difficulty),
            seed=int(seed),
            gold_objective=float(gold_objective),
            baseline_objective=float(baseline_objective),
            value_cap=float(value_cap),
            components=normalized_components,
        )
        final_submission_source, final_evaluation = evaluate_final_submission(
            row,
            instance=inst,
            cls=str(class_),
            components=normalized_components,
        )
        gap_pct = final_evaluation.get("gap_pct")
        if gap_pct is None:
            gap_pct = final_evaluation.get("details", {}).get("gap_pct")
        detail = {
            "id": str(id),
            "class": str(class_),
            "difficulty": str(difficulty),
            "seed": int(seed),
            "score": float(row["score"]),
            "score_at_stop": float(row["score_at_stop"]),
            "score_after_cf": float(row["score_after_cf"]),
            "cf_delta": float(row["cf_delta"]),
            "stop_reason": row["stop_reason"],
            "wall_s": float(row["wall_s"]),
            "n_exec_turns": int(row["n_exec_turns"]),
            "error": row["error"],
            "parsed": row["parsed"],
            "cf_parsed": row["cf_parsed"],
            "transcript": row["transcript"],
            "parse_path": infer_parse_path(row),
            "parse_events": row.get("parse_events"),
            "feasible": infer_feasible(final_evaluation),
            "final_submission_source": final_submission_source,
            "final_evaluation": final_evaluation,
            "gap_pct": gap_pct,
        }
        detail_rows.append(detail)
        return float(row["score"])
